originalTitle: Nicaragua's legislature debates barring opposition from elections
summary: The move comes days after President Daniel Ortega demanded 'a wall, a blockade' against opposition parties.
content:
Nicaragua's government-controlled National Assembly is debating whether to bar opposition parties from running in elections and will vote on the matter in the first week of August.

The move comes just days after President Daniel Ortega said that there "will be no more elections for them [the opposition] to try to seize the government, to seize power".

His remarks were condemned by UN High Commissioner for Human Rights Volker Türk who said that "people of all political viewpoints must be allowed to vote and run for office".

Ortega, 80, has been in power since 2007. His rule has become increasingly authoritarian, with political opponents jailed, sent into exile and stripped of their citizenship.

Ortega made the controversial remarks on Sunday in a speech marking the anniversary of the 1979 Sandinista revolution, when the left-wing group he was part of removed the US-backed dictator Anastasio Somoza from power.

In his speech, Ortega accused the opposition, which he said was financed by the US, of "conspiring, trying to organise parties to try and win an election".

He said laws would be passed "to create a wall, to block the coup mongers", which he said would ensure that "regardless of how much money they are given by the Yankees", they would not seize power.

Among those who condemned Ortega's words was US Secretary of State Marco Rubio.

He said they laid bare the "true authoritarian nature" of Ortega's rule.

Rubio added that the Nicaraguan leader and his wife, Rosario Murillo, whom Ortega named as his "co-president" in February 2025, had "abandoned even the pretense of popular consent".

But the head of Nicaragua's National Assembly, Gustavo Porras, dismissed the criticism in an interview with a government-aligned news site.

He said that the legislative body and Nicaragua's highest electoral authority were debating how "to not leave even a crack" for the opposition to establish a foothold.

Presidential elections were last held in Nicaragua in November 2021.

The one-sided contest was won by Ortega following a brutal crackdown on the opposition.

Any rival potential candidates with a chance to garner a significant number of votes were detained and disqualified from standing for office or forced into exile. Dozens of other opposition members were also arrested.

Only a small handful of little-known candidates, who were unlikely to get much support, were allowed to stand.

In November 2025, when Ortega was four years into his five-year term, the National Assembly passed a constitutional reform extending the presidential term to six years.

Under the new system, which was passed unanimously by the National Assembly, Rosario Murillo also became "co-president".

It paved the way for her to assume the role of president should Ortega, who is believed to be in frail health, should be declared "permanently absent".

Few Nicaraguans have dared to speak out in public against Ortega and Murillo since more than 300 people were killed when the security forces quashed anti-government protests in 2018.

Nicaraguan opposition politicians living in exile, however, roundly decried Ortega's latest plans. Juan Sebastián Chamorro said it was proof of Ortega's desire to "turn Nicaragua into a one-party country like China, North Korea, and Cuba".

Felix Maradiaga, a former presidential candidate for the opposition who was jailed under Ortega's rule, told AFP news agency that it showed the leader was afraid of dissent: "Ortega is admitting what most worries him: he hasn't neutralised the opposition, he knows the desire for change is still alive, and is still fighting for the country."

chineseTitle: 尼加拉瓜立法机构辩论禁止反对派参加选举
chineseSummary: 几天前，总统丹尼尔·奥尔特加要求对反对党“建墙、封锁”。
chineseContent:
尼加拉瓜政府控制的国民议会正在讨论是否禁止反对党参加选举，并将在八月的第一周就此事进行投票。

就在几天前，总统丹尼尔·奥尔特加表示，“他们（反对派）将不再举行选举来试图夺取政府、夺取权力”。

他的言论遭到联合国人权事务高级专员沃尔克·蒂尔克的谴责，并表示“必须允许持不同政治观点的人投票和竞选公职”。

现年 80 岁的奥尔特加自 2007 年以来一直掌权。他的统治变得越来越独裁，政治对手被监禁、流放并被剥夺公民身份。

周日，奥尔特加在纪念 1979 年桑地诺革命周年纪念的演讲中发表了这一颇具争议的言论，当时他所属的左翼组织将美国支持的独裁者阿纳斯塔西奥·索摩查赶下台。

奥尔特加在讲话中指责反对派“密谋，试图组织政党以赢得选举”，他称反对派是由美国资助的。

他表示，将通过法律“建造一堵墙，阻止政变贩子”，他说这将确保“无论洋基队给他们多少钱”，他们都不会夺取权力。

谴责奥尔特加言论的人包括美国国务卿马可·卢比奥。

他说，他们暴露了奥尔特加统治的“真正的独裁性质”。

卢比奥补充说，这位尼加拉瓜领导人和他的妻子罗萨里奥·穆里略（Ortega 于 2025 年 2 月任命为他的“联合总统”）“甚至放弃了民众同意的假装”。

但尼加拉瓜国民议会议长古斯塔沃·波拉斯在接受与政府结盟的新闻网站采访时驳斥了这些批评。

他说，立法机构和尼加拉瓜最高选举当局正在讨论如何“不留下一丝缝隙”让反对派立足。

尼加拉瓜上次选举于 2021 年 11 月举行。

奥尔特加在残酷镇压反对派后赢得了这场一边倒的比赛。

任何有机会获得大量选票的竞争对手的潜在候选人都会被拘留，并被取消竞选公职的资格或被迫流亡。数十名其他反对派成员也被捕。

只有少数鲜为人知且不太可能获得太多支持的候选人被允许参选。

2025年11月，奥尔特加五年任期已满第四年，国民议会通过了宪法改革，将总统任期延长至六年。

根据国民议会一致通过的新制度，罗萨里奥·穆里略也成为“联合总统”。

如果据信健康状况不佳的奥尔特加被宣布“永久缺席”，这为她担任总统铺平了道路。

自 2018 年安全部队镇压反政府抗议活动导致 300 多人死亡以来，很少有尼加拉瓜人敢于公开反对奥尔特加和穆里略。

然而，流亡中的尼加拉瓜反对派政客严厉谴责了奥尔特加的最新计划。胡安·塞巴斯蒂安·查莫罗表示，这证明了奥尔特加希望“将尼加拉瓜变成像中国、朝鲜和古巴一样的一党制国家”。

在奥尔特加统治下被监禁的反对派前总统候选人菲利克斯·马拉迪亚加告诉法新社，这表明这位领导人害怕异议：“奥尔特加承认了他最担心的事情：他没有压制反对派，他知道变革的愿望仍然存在，并且仍在为国家而战。”

尼加拉瓜原住民领袖入狱三年后去世

美国确保尼加拉瓜释放 135 名政治犯

奥尔特加加强对尼加拉瓜“哑剧”投票的控制
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
